content: - Alvero et al. (2025) AJ Alvero, Dustin S. Stoltz, Oscar Stuhler, and Marshall A. Taylor. Generative ai in sociological research: State of the discipline. Sociological Science, 2025. Forthcoming; preprint available at arXiv:2511.16884.

- Bakker et al. (2015) Bert N Bakker, Kokil Jaidka, Timothy Dörr, Neil Fasching, and Yphtach Lelkes. Questionable and open research practices: Attitudes and perceptions among quantitative communication researchers. Journal of Communication, 71(5):715–738, 2021.
- BALDWIN (2015) Melinda Baldwin. Credibility, peer review, and Nature, 1945–1990. Notes and Records of the Royal Society, 69(3):337–352, 2015. doi: 10.1098/rsnr.2015.0029.
- BALDWIN (2018) Melinda Baldwin. Peer review. In Encyclopedia of the History of Science. Carnegie Mellon University, 2018. URL https://ethos.lps.library.cmu.edu/article/id/19/.

- Bekkers et al. (2025) René Bekkers, Daniel Lakens, Lisa DeBruine, Cristian Mesquida Caldenty, and M. Littel. Research transparency check. Open Science Framework (OSF) project, 2025. URL https://osf.io/z3tr9. Project to develop software that provides an automatic assessment and suggestions for the improvement of the transparency of data and methods in research reports.

- Bengio et al. (2025) Yoshua Bengio, Sören Mindermann, Daniel Privitera, Tamay Besiroglu, Rishi Bommasani, Stephen Casper, Yejin Choi, et al. International AI safety report 2025. Technical report, AI Safety Institute, January 2025. URL https://internationalaisafetyreport.org/.

- Berti et al. (2025) Leonardo Berti, Luca Palmieri, and Maria Sofia Bucarelli. Emergent abilities in large language models: A survey. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.05788, March 2025. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.05788.

- Chakravorti et al. (2025) Tatiana Chakravorti, Xinyu Wang, Pranav Narayanan Venkit, Sai Koneru, Kevin Munger, and Sarah Rajtmajer. Social scientists on the role of ai in research. Proceedings of the Eighth AAAI/ACM Conference on AI, Ethics and Society, pages 528–540, 2025.
- Collins and Evans (2007) Harry Collins and Robert Evans. Rethinking expertise. University of Chicago press, 2007.
- Corten and Lawrence (2021) Corinna Cortes and Neil D. Lawrence. Inconsistency in conference peer review: Revisiting the 2014 NeurIPS experiment. arXiv preprint arXiv:2109.09774, 2021.

Jamie Cummins, Beth Clarke, Ian Hussey, and Malte Elson. Regcheck: A tool for automating comparisons between study registrations and papers. arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.13330, 2026. doi: 10.48550/arXiv.2601.13330. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.13330.

- Dolan and L. Lawless (2024) Kathleen Dolan and Jennifer L. Lawless. American journal of political science: Annual report to the executive council of the midwest political science association. Technical Report, 2024.

- W. G. E. (2024) Edutopia. Why using AI is fine for teachers but not for students. Edutopia, October 2025. URL https://www.edutopia.org/article/why-students-should-not-use-ai/. Accessed January 2026.

- H. H. (2026) David H. Guston and Kenneth Keniston. Introduction: The social contract for science. In David H. Guston and Kenneth Keniston, editors, The Fragile Contract: University Science and the Federal Government, pages 1–41. MIT Press, Cambridge, MA, 1994.
- Hao et al. (2026) Qianyue Hao, Fengli Xu, Yong Li, and James Evans. Artificial intelligence tools expand scientists’ impact but contract science’s focus. Nature, pages 1–7, 2026.
- J. J. (2005) Thomas J Hostler. The invisible workload of open research. Journal of Trial & Error, 4(1):21–36, 2023.
- E. E. (2022) Sven E Hug. Towards theorizing peer review. Quantitative Science Studies, 3(3):815–831, 2022.
- K. E. (2005) Daniel Kennefick. Einstein versus the physical review. Physics Today, 58(9):43–48, 2005. doi: 10.1063/1.2117822.
- K. E. (2023) Anton Korinek. Scenario planning for an a (g) i future. IMF Finance & Development Magazine, 60(4):30–33, 2023.
- G. G. (2023) Diana Kwon. Scientists split on ethics of ai use. Nature, 641:574–577, 2025. doi: 10.1038/d41586-025-01463-8.
- G. G. (2023) Regina G. Lawrence. Editor’s note jan 2025. Political Communication, 42(1):1–5, 2025. doi: 10.1080/10584609.2024.2444101.

- L. G. (2023) Weixin Liang, Mert Yuksekgonul, Yining Mao, Eric Wu, and James Zou. GPT detectors are biased against non-native English writers. Patterns, 4(7):100779, 2023. doi: 10.1016/j.patter.2023.100779.

- L. G. (2023) Weixin Liang, Yaohui Zhang, Zhengxuan Wu, Haley Lepp, Wenlong Ji, Xuandong Zhao, Hancheng Cao, Sheng Liu, Siyu He, Zhi Huang, et al. Quantifying large language model usage in scientific papers. Nature Human Behaviour, pages 1–11, 2025.

Jake Linardon, Hannah K. Jarman, Zoe McClure, Cleo Anderson, Claudia Liu, and Mariel Messer. Influence of topic familiarity and prompt specificity on citation fabrication in mental health research using large language models: Experimental study. JMIR Mental Health, 12:e80371, November 2025. doi: 10.2196/80371. Found that 19.9% of GPT-4o citations were entirely fabricated and 45.4% of real citations contained errors; fabrication rates varied by topic familiarity (6% for depression vs 28-29% for less-known disorders).
Noah Moxham and Aileen Fyfe. The Royal Society and the prehistory of peer review, 1665–1965. The Historical Journal, 61(4):863–889, 2018. doi: 10.1017/S0018246X17000334.

Jeremy Y Ng, Laura Kelly, Lesley Ogilvie, and David Moher. Attitudes and perceptions of medical researchers towards the use of artificial intelligence chatbots in the scientific process: an international cross-sectional survey. Lancet Digital Health, 7:e94–e102, 2025. doi: 10.1016/S2589-7500(24)00202-4. Published online November 15, 2024.
Nicholas G. Otis, Katelyn Cranney, Solène Delecourt, and Rembrand Koning. Global evidence on gender gaps and generative AI, 2024. URL https://ideas.repec.org/p/osf/osfxxx/h6a7c.html.
Alexis Palmer, Noah A Smith, and Arthur Spirling. Using proprietary language models in academic research requires explicit justification. Nature Computational Science, 4(1):2–3, 2024.

Vinu Sankar Sadasivan, Aounon Kumar, Sriram Balasubramanian, Wenxiao Wang, and Soheil Feizi. Can AI-generated text be reliably detected? Transactions on Machine Learning Research, 2024. URL https://openreview.net/forum?id=NvSwR4IvLO. Originally arXiv:2303.11156.
Nazar Shmatko, Alex Adam, and Paul Esau. Gptzero finds 100 new hallucinations in neurips 2025 accepted papers, January 2026. URL https://gptzero.me/news/neurips/. Accessed: 2026-01-22.
Richard Smith. Peer review: a flawed process at the heart of science and journals. Journal of the royal society of medicine, 99(4):178–182, 2006.
Max Tegmark. Life 3.0: Being human in the age of artificial intelligence. Vintage, 2018.
Jonathan P Tennant and Tony Ross-Hellauer. The limitations to our understanding of peer review. Research integrity and peer review, 5(1):6, 2020.

Mike Thelwall and Pardeep Sud. Scopus 1900–2020: Growth in articles, abstracts, countries, fields, and journals. Quantitative Science Studies, 3(1): 37–50, April 2022. ISSN 2641-3337. doi: 10.1162/qssa00177.

Andrew Tomkins, Min Zhang, and William D. Heavlin. Reviewer bias in single-versus double-blind peer review. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 114(48):12708–12713, 2017. doi: 10.1073/pnas.1707323114.
- Tripp and Dion (2024) Aili Tripp and Michelle Dion. American political science review annual editorial report. Technical Report, 2024.

- Teachers (2025) Vice. Teachers are using AI to grade papers—while banning students from it. Vice, April 2025. URL https://www.vice.com/en/article/teachers-are-using-ai-to-grade-papers-while-banning-students-from-it/. Accessed January 2026.

- Villalobos et al. (2024) Pablo Villalobos, Anson Ho, Jaime Sevilla, Tamay Besiroglu, Lennart Heim, and Marius Hobbhahn. Position: Will we run out of data? limits of LLM scaling based on human-generated data. In Proceedings of the 41st International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), pages 49523–49544, 2024. URL https://proceedings.mlr.press/v235/villalobos24a.html.

- Walters and Walter (2023) William H. Walters and Esther Isabelle Wilder. Fabrication and errors in the bibliographic citations generated by ChatGPT. Scientific Reports, 13(1):14045, September 2023. doi: 10.1038/s41598-023-41032-5.

- Wolfe (2018) Audra J. Wolfe. Sticky policies, dysfunctional systems: Path dependency and the problems of government funding for science in the United States. Minerva, 56(4):479–503, 2018. doi: 10.1007/s11024-018-9352-0.
